author: @iami9307
text: I got a three wheel small child’s bike from the trash. It had a 24 V motor on each wheel with a gearbox built on to each one. All I had to do was mount one and put a hand crank on it and it generated considerable Power. I mounted the other on the back rack of a bicycle and it assisted in peddling or it would charge the battery.

author: @reniwatson8609
text: I can remember this one gadget that my Dad built and never bothered to explain how it worked. It was three eye hole screws, one on top of the other, a wire going to each one, and what made the whole thing work was dropping water into the common holes that were lined up. I remember Mom saying that She didn't understand it either but that He does that kind of thing often. My question ; is water dropping through a few holes an actual power generator or not?

replies.author: @iami9307
replies.text: Glad you shared that with everyone. You brought up something I forgot about. There has been various attempts to trickle water down wires and through pipes but resulting in nano volts and not practical or usable. Of course that was then but today electronics are so Advanced that might be worth looking into again.

replies.author: @iami9307
replies.text: How it works. Everything has ions. In fact the air you stand in is a semi solid. It’s made up of gases, water, and dust particles which all contain ions. The water droplets which contain ions which strip the air of more ions as it fals. Hope that helps you to understand.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text: That sounds like a tiny version of a "bladeless wind turbine" that was shown in some YT video few years back. Put that in the search term and pick the video with a picture of a kind of square frame, IIRC it was being tested by some university or company but I don't remember seeing any news of it after that.

author: @nw7696
text: Awesome! When GM had the power steering motor recalls, dealerships were literally throwing them out by the hundreds! Extremely high torque 12vdc motors with a max unloaded speed of +/- 3000 RPM, they also make very good generators. The 11 tooth gear on the end was made out of a type of sintered steel (not really drillable or  weldable), but it can be pressed off easily enough.

author: @FloatingIdeasonanarrowboat
text: Buck convert to 5V at around 8A and charge a lifepo4 battery. Then boost the battery to 5V and for every minute of cranking your battery would give 16 minutes charging at 500ma.

replies.author: @TrollFaceTheMan
replies.text:
Got to keep in mind compounding losses there.

 The buck, charge then boost again would probably lose between 20% on the low end of the power. And if not done very efficiently maybe closer to 40%.

BRAVO….   hook this thing up to a stationary bicycle and you really have something we all can use…….

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thank you mate and you are spot on this would make a great bike genny

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
When I was 12 there was some kind of technology expo at nearby school and one of the exhibits was an exercise bike with a bunch of lamps so you could test how many watts you can pedal out of it, I think I got 40, for a few seconds at least. I wasn't exactly an athletic kid, but you could use it for temporary emergency power.

Couple years ago someone commented on a video about solar power claiming it would be cheaper and easier to power their house with pedal generators, so I helpfully pointed out that assuming you could get a generous but plausible 100W out of the generator and could hire people to pedal them for $5/h your cost of electricity would be $50/kWh...

An average American household uses about 30kWh/day. Even if you could make the generators for free, it would take less than a month for the cost of feeding your slaves to pass the one time cost of installing domestic solar and batteries. :D

author: @TabooRevolution13
text: Now for the World's best bicycle generator challenge!    I made mine with a 4.5hp tredmill motor!   A wind blocking diode...

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: What kind of power did you get and how did you use it? 90 or 180VDC?

replies.author: @michaelcasper4727
replies.text: Got you covered - I did the bike generator video for Robert's printer contest.  18 speed bike, DC 110 Volt motor (generator), cement flywheel and fan belt.  The Electrolytic caps can store 70-80 volts and using a buck volt converter - lit a single LED.  So, I can generate about 70-80 watts, but I am no Lance Armstrong.

replies.author: @TabooRevolution13
replies.text: @michaelcasper4727 I power my phone and my laptop with mine on a regular basis. Making a concrete wheel would be a perk... I have 4 500f capacitors in series and I toy with them too.

replies.author: @TabooRevolution13
replies.text: @Teknopottu Mine puts out DC power for the battery bank at 9-32 volts.

replies.author: @michaelcasper4727
replies.text: @TabooRevolution13 I do it the other way - I charge the 5.3 Amp hour 15-volt ultracapacitor bank and then use the bank for lighting the upstairs and phone charging.  Robert did a video on the flywheel, it does smooth out the pedaling, eliminates generator torque vibration and stores a second's worth of power.

author: @DoubleBob
text:
5:12 " you're not going to be able to charge your phone"
How much power would it require to charge a phone or a laptop? Would leg-muscles do it? How do the portable mini-solar panels charge phones, if so much muscle strength is insufficient?
On another note: Would it be possible to power a mini-fridge with that?

replies.author: @angelusmendez5084
replies.text: I think he meant, "not [only] be able to charge your phone..."

replies.author: @iami9307
replies.text: That will charge all sorts of batteries but it will need a capacitor and a regulator to match whatever is being charged. A mini fridge would have to be ran off of a battery or an inverter connected to a battery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it was a joke mate - of course you could charge your phone

author: @dwaynewladyka577
text: That was quite impressive. Can you modify a hand crank flashlight, with a 1 farad electrolytic capacitor, so it doesn't require a battery? Cheers!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate - that would be pretty easy to be honest

replies.author: @mikebond6328
replies.text: I’ve taken a few hand crank flashlights apart and they contained super capacitors like you would find in cameras for the flash.

replies.author: @dwaynewladyka577
replies.text: @mikebond6328 I have a crank powered flashlight, which has around 16 LEDs in it. It is shaped like a hockey puck. I bought it at a drugstore, for around $10 Canadian. The light doesn't last very long. It does have a charger for the battery too. I'd like to replace the battery with a 1 farad, or 1.5 farad electrolytic capacitor, and make it last longer. Cheers!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @dwaynewladyka577 A super capacitor would better suit your application.  Electrolytic capacitors want to release their stored charge as fast as possible.  Easier to get a smooth flow out of a super capacitor for longer discharge.  But a good idea none the less.

replies.author: @dwaynewladyka577
replies.text: @jamesross1003 Thanks!
